comments.title: Speaking Well and Middle/Neo-Platonism In Wizard Robes
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To those who mark it, a Happy Easter, and for those who don't, may the rebirth of the world this spring bring you many good things anyhow!

To Share: If anyone is interested in getting better at public speaking, I've posted my thoughts in two parts here: https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2026/03/04/speaking-well-part-1/

and here: https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2026/03/20/speaking-well-part-2/

(By way of qualification, I had to do a lot of public speaking in my former career as a management consultant and have been teaching it at the undergraduate and MBA level for the last eight years. If all that business school stuff puts you off, please note that my point with my current blog project is precisely to address what I see as the shortcomings of the typical attitude in business education.)

To Ask: I enjoyed your recent appearance on Therapeutic Astrology, and one of your comments struck me as especially interesting, since I'm currently reading Dillon's The Middle Platonists: you said that "occultism is just Middle Platonism dressed up in wizard robes" (or something very like that). I had been under the impression that occultism was more Neo-Platonism in wizard robes, drawing heavily on folks like Plotinus and Proclus. So, my question is whether you think that's an especially meaningful distinction, and if so, what makes occultism more like Middle Platonism than Neoplatonism?

And a follow-up question: on the back of Dillon's book are some books I take to be similar surveys of other times/schools, Neoplatonism by R. Wallis and The Presocratics by E. Hussey - do you happen to know if these are comparably good as Dillon?

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Speaking Well and Middle/Neo-Platonism In Wizard Robes
replies.content: Hermeticism, Gnosticism, and Cabala are all derived from Middle Platonism rather than Neoplatonism. Neoplatonism had a strong influence on mysticism by way of Pseudo-Dionysius, but Iamblichean theurgy didn't really start to have an effect on later occultism until the 19th century, after Thomas Taylor made the core texts available in translation. I read the book by Wallis a long time ago and don't recall how good it was; I haven't read Hussey's book.

replies.title: Re: Speaking Well and Middle/Neo-Platonism In Wizard Robes
replies.content:
Ah, I see, thanks very much! It has been interesting reading through descriptions of various bits of Middle Platonism and going "that sounds awfully familiar."

And thanks as well for the thoughts on the books, if I end up reading them, I'll share thoughts here.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Incarnation?
comments.content: When I was a child I remember several times drifting awake in the middle of the night with the same peculiar feeling.  Very peaceful...just floating.  My body feels insubstantial.  My head feels sort of puffy and amorphous.  My lips feel very sensitive and important, like the very center of consciousness.  And it felt more like a memory than a dream.  Do you think a person can remember the time of incarnation in their mother's womb?  Even as a child, I wondered if that was a echo of my first moments of personhood.

replies.title: Re: Incarnation?
replies.content: I know people who remember their first hours of life outside the womb; for that matter, I have a memory that's probably from that, though I can't be sure. (It's mostly lying on my back, feeling very cold and lonely, and seeing something pale and gray and far away that was probably a ceiling seen through eyes that didn't focus well yet.) So it's by no means impossible.

replies.title: Re: Incarnation?
replies.content: Your memory prompted me to share my memory of giving birth to my second son.  For a fleeting moment I was looking down at myself on the bed. I made some commentary to myself about the state of my body. After my son was born I asked my husband if there has been a mirror on the ceiling in the room or a television screen. There was nothing there. So, I watched my son being born for a fleeting moment.  My life was never at risk (my vitals were fine and I was healthy during the process)

comments.title: Inviting a patron (saint) for an organisation
comments.content:
How does an organisation invite/petition a (deceased) heroic figure to be our patron (saint)? How do we know if the person accepts? Through divination?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Inviting a patron (saint) for an organisation
replies.content: Start by doing a divination to see if the spirit is amenable. If you get a "yes," do a ceremony with an image of the person, describing the person's heroic deeds and formally making them the patron of the organization. Follow up with an annual event commemorating the person on their birthday, or the day of some particularly glorious deed. Go from there. The initial divination gives you permission; the rest is about building a relationship.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
A bit of a question on a strange situation with a lot of grief.

My dog passed away twelve days ago. He was the last of a rescue batch shipped up from a shelter in California ten and a half years ago. He picked me out and was afraid of everyone else. It was only bit later I remembered a very strong dream from about twenty years previous and he was the dog in it. Definitely, he was very unique and I expect finding another dog like him is only slightly more likely than finding the Ring of Eibon in a Happy Meal. We were very close.

The night before he passed, (I found a vet able to make house calls) I had one of those vivid dreams. I was in the living room where we were both sleeping in real life. The details were out of body vivid, not dream like. There were two of him. His friendly and happy self greeting his older scared self. Another dog of mine who had passed years ago also showed up as if he was a guide.

Last night, twelve days after the last dream, I had another. It was brief. I saw my dog running and happy. Instead of lifelike and vivid like the last one, this one was ghostly. I think it was a reassurance he was okay. (I hope)

I had gotten another dog. Divinations (if I read them correctly) said it would be best to adopt from who was left after the free adoption day at the shelter. That was on Good Friday.

The new dog is a real sweetheart and she's trying to make it work out too. The grief is still there. There's been a few times walking past my dog's grave or along where we used to walk, I've broken down sobbing.

The strange thing is that this evening (Easter) a bit after sunset, I noticed there was a piece of snowflake obsidian centered on top of the pellet stove. It was off the shelf nearby and could not have fallen there. I am the only one who could have reached it, and I know I hadn't touched that shelf in a couple of weeks.

Handling the stone and keeping it nearby does help. Is the case where objects around the house are being moved more the work of household spirits than deities? If so, what is the best way to thank them? I am deeply grateful and doubt I would want to offend anyone who has helped like that anyways.

Hope that made sense.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It makes perfect sense. First of all, please accept my condolences on your loss. I know how difficult that sort of thing can be.

Second, the dreams are entirely typical, and all traditional lore indicates that they mean what you think they mean. The soul of your late dog is fine.

Third, apportation of small objects is another fairly common event in situations like this. No, it wasn't household spirits or gods; the soul of your late dog will have done it. (The souls of the recently dead can sometimes do such things, if they have the motivation.) Take it as a gift.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you so very much for this. It means a great deal.

What made the dream about him back in the mid 90s before I got him so memorable, was the strong, almost aching feeling of love. It was a very special bond.

I am just grateful he was able to live out his life and I was able to stay with him at home in the end. He would freak out if I left him with someone else for even a few minutes. The grief has been overwhelming at times, but I would much rather suffer that than being the one who left him.

It will now be prayers of gratitude for his safe passage and listening carefully for what needs to be done next.

comments.title: Past-life Practice Hints
comments.content:
Given that people are expected to take multiple lifetimes of spiritual practice before they're done with incarnation, are there any accepted signs that you did magic in previous lives? I was never drawn to conventional religious thinking but felt an instant affinity and familiarity once I discovered magical literature. I take naturally to most meditative and energetic practices and divination methods I've tried; I was getting useful tarot readings within a week of starting when apparently some people take years. Ever since I was little I would give myself "impromptu willpower tests" where I would randomly resolve to do things like not look at clocks for the rest of the school day. It was interesting to compare these activities to methods of occult training like your "Training the Will" post series. Why would I decide to do that on a whim?

Another thing, the event that really sent me down the road of magical practice was creative work that led me to what could probably be described as contact with the sphere of Harmony/Tiphareth. I only achieved this contact for a moment but it transformed my understanding of my artistic field and the unifying concepts behind it. Does this contact prior to any magical practice suggest that I did some kind of pathworking in a prior incarnation?

Lastly, do you feel that there are definite reasons you shouldn't use computer files for a magical journal? I recall someone saying something like that. That's what I've been doing and journaling has been one of the biggest turning points in my practice, helping me go from one missed day a week on average to less than one missed day of practice a month, with make-up work after any missed day. Because of that I'm reluctant to switch to a more laborious method of journaling. The electronic journal also makes it easy to search for past divinations by topic.

Thanks for these posts.

replies.title: Re: Past-life Practice Hints
replies.content:
1) It's hard to say whether the sort of thing you've described are signs that you've done magical training in the past, or simply that you came into this life knowing that there were certain kinds of training you need to do. (In my case, for example, it was some of both.) You may well have done some magical training in a previous life -- many people have, you know. The thing to watch for is a cascade of "coincidences" that puts you into contact with some specific occult tradition. That normally happens if you were initiated into that tradition in one life, and are ready to return to it in this one.

2) That's entirely a matter of personal preference. If you'd rather use a computer, by all means.

replies.title: Re: How to find a healing spirit/god/deity?
replies.content:
It's important to remember that you have neither the right nor the power to make a deity or spirit show up to help you. The initiative has to come from their side, and it will come when the deity or spirit decides the time is right.

What you can do is let them know that you're open to working with them. For example, before you begin a massage treatment, silently pray: "If there are beings of positive intent who wish to help heal this person, I invite their presence and participation." You'll know the offer has been taken up when you feel healing energies flowing through you, or when intuitive guidance starts directing your work, or both.

I had something like this happen when I had to travel to NYC and extract my mother from a crowded, chaotic hospital where she ended up after a medical emergency while traveling. The hospital was dangerously overcrowded and the staff completely overwhelmed and overworked, with terrible communication between nurses and doctors. I decided as I entered that I would invoke my highest self in every encounter while there, and attempt to contact the other person’s as well. For example, before dealing with a highly stressed and annoyed charge nurse, whose cooperation I needed to contact the doctor assigned to my mother, I would silently say, “Highest self of mine, please greet this person’s highest self and ask for help.” And then—crucially—I forced myself to believe this was possible and could be done, that us strangers could deal Highest-Self-to-Highest-Self.

It unequivocally worked. Every single person we dealt with seemed to bring their best to the circumstances, and went out of their way to help.

There were other oddities. One nurse suddenly broke down in sobs when I said, in passing but with sincerity, “You are asked to do too much, more than any one person can handle.” I really deeply felt compassion for him. It was surprising but seemed related.

Anyway, all of this is hard to put into words…but the effects were very real.

comments.content:
Hey JMG,

1) I've been wondering about agalmata. The theory seems simple enough—take a hollow statue, put stuff associated with the deity in question into it, and just start using it as an image for altar prayer, if I got that right.

But beyond that, the process isn't clear to me. Is every material fine? Burnt clay would be naturally hollow but you mentioned using wood statues—so are those just regular wood carvings hollowed out? Where does the hole go? I'd assume on the bottom for aesthetics but then you'd have to plug it with something.

2) On that note, and I'm both asking you and the commentariat, where do you get your altar statues? Do you make them yourself? Commission an artist? Etsy?

3) You said that the LBRP will deconsecrate agalmata. I don't particularly like the idea of putting them into a bag, nor shrouding them every time. I was instead thinking about giving each statue an open box lined with silk or linen, with drapes at the front. Does that sound sensible?

4) On an unrelated note, I've skimmed parts of Franz Bardon's Initiation into Hermetics. If I understand you correctly, you think that Bardon's system works well, it's just not the one you've chosen.

I was a bit surprised that Bardon includes neither divination nor banishing (discursive meditation, to complete the trifecta, is clearly there, even if the focus is on the mind-emptying kind of meditation). But then I started wondering about the following passage:

Hand in hand with the inner development of spirit and soul has to go that of the outer, the body also. No part of your Ego must lag behind or be neglected. Right in the morning, after getting up, you will brush your body with a soft brush until your skin turns faintly reddish. By doing so, your pores will open and be able to breathe more freely. Besides, the kidneys are exonerated for the most part. Then wash your whole body or the upper part of it, at least, with cold water and rub it with a rough towel until you feel quite warm. Sensitive people may use lukewarm water, especially in the cold season. This procedure ought to become a day's routine and be kept for a lifetime. It is so refreshing and removes tiredness.


As cold water is a good etheric conductor, would this fulfil the purpose of a daily banishing, i.e. removing etheric crud?

—David P.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Since the art of making and using agalmata is still being rediscovered, there's a lot that isn't yet known about it. I have no idea, for example, what effect different materials will have. The ancients used stone, bronze, and wood, and maybe other materials; ceramics such as fired clay would likely work well, but you'd have to experiment. Mine are wooden; they have 1 inch diameter holes drilled in the bottom, extending up much of the way through the image, and wooden plugs glued in place to seal the holes.

2) I got mine made by friends who are good at woodcarving. Since mine are Druid deities, the carvings are very simple -- they look as though they were made in a tribal setting a couple of thousand years ago!

3) You could do that, or simply drape a piece of silk or linen over them when you do your LBRP.

4) Yes, the water practice has a banishing effect, and so does his method of breathing energies into and out of the body -- that purifies the etheric and astral bodies.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you. That sounds a lot simpler than I imagined it.

4) With a regular LBRP or SoP practice, you said that one also cleanses the nearby environment (hence the need for silk/linen in the case of the LBRP). Would it be correct to assume that Bardon's methods don't confer that advantage?

—David P.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's correct. The emergence of banishing rituals as a basic practice in occult training was one of the great magical innovations of the 19th century, and it's turned out to be an extremely valuable addition to the traditions.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks. While we're on the subject of Bardon, I have another question. He makes quite a number of very grandiose claims in Initiation into Hermetics. Those who "specialize more particularly in the use of the elements" are allegedly able to learn quite a number of feats. From what you've mentioned about etheric energies and what humans can do with them, most of them lie on a spectrum between those that I could see being possible with years of practice (such as imperviousness to pain or paralysing others in a fight) and those that seem just barely credible for a true master (such as levitation, seeing as you said that etheric telekinesis is possible).

Beyond the latter end of the spectrum falls what he says about the element of fire:

With the help of the fire element the magician is able to stand the greatest cold if he accumulates this element in himself. The lamas in Tiber can produce an enormous heat in their bodies with the help of the accumulated fire element, so that they can quickly dry wet sheets wrapped on their bodies in the middle of winter. This experiment is known as "tumo" in Tibet. Any kind of combustible material can be ignited with the outward-projected fire element.


Especially the last part, setting things on fire, seems to fall squarely under Rowling's Law—I've read it in a Harry Potter novel and thus it's not real magic.

So what gives? Is Bardon exaggerating here or is it actually possible to etherically set something on fire?

(Before you tell me off again, I am explicitly not asking for instructions, just whether or not it's possible.)

—David P.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I have never seen anybody light a fire using magic, and I doubt that it's possible.

On the other hand, tummo is apparently quite real. People who traveled in Tibet before the Chinese takeover describe young monks having tummo contests in which they'd go out in midwinter dressed only in loincloths, break the ice on the nearest watercourse, dip sheets into the nearly freezing water, and see who could cause the largest number of sheets to steam themselves dry by wrapping them around their bodies. The human metabolism is capable of quite a bit of heat output; of course it helps that winters in Tibet are very dry as well as very cold, and a modest amount of heat will cause water vapor to be absorbed by the extremely dry air.

The only examples of etheric telekinesis I've ever witnessed, by the way, involved making very light and well-balanced objects move. I'd be astounded if anybody could levitate a human body.

replies.title: Re: A Question about the subtle bodies

I.e. if I were you, I'd closely watch the "weeds" which come up the most along the fence - and I'd consider giving them some space to work there, to the extent possible (i.e. without offending other people's sensitivities, and without letting them take over the garden ;-) ).

It seems, generally speaking, that good material insulators make good etheric insulators. I recently watched a Townsends video on Youtube about making a wattle fence (stick posts in the ground, and then weave flexible sticks around them). To make more permanent structures, a common medieval technique was "waddle and daub" - build the wattle as usual by weaving sticks, then coat in "daub" - clay-ey mud plus something fibrous (straw, thread, horse manure, et cetera). Such a combo would certainly qualify as materially-insulating, and might work etherically as well - you could build such structures parallel to the plastic fencing, a fence/wall within a fence is how I imagine it.

Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)

Once you can feel them, accept them, and acknowledge that the way you were treated was monstrously wrong and that your feelings were wholly appropriate at the time, you can let them flow out of you and reclaim the strengths you had to lock away from yourself back then.

comments.title: Ogham Interpretation
comments.content:
Thank you, as always, for MM.

I am teaching myself to read the Ogham. I am curious to know if you or the commentariat determined the best way to interpret a yes or no question.

My ability is improving and this question has me a little stuck.

replies.title: Re: Ogham Interpretation
replies.content: I always did it intuitively, with an eye toward the details of the question. I know that probably doesn't help you at all!

replies.title: Re: Ogham Interpretation
replies.content:
I also found this somewhat challenging and usually tried to follow JMG's approach for the most part. I also tended to interpret reversed cards as more likely to mean "no" and upright to mean "yes."

In The Runecaster's Handbook, Edred Thorsson recommended an approach with Runestaves for yes/no questions: set out a cloth with a central ring with a diameter of ~8 inches marked on it (or visualize such a ring), take up the staves, think about your question, close your eyes, and let them fall on the cloth. Looking only at the staves that fell in the ring, count the number that are face up ("bright") and the number that are face-down ("murky") - more brights than murkies means "Yes," and vice versa means "no." Thorsson cautions that this approach works better if you've already gotten comfortable with the Runes for more interpretative questions, so it's not purely a matter of mechanics. I've had some success with this approach, but I almost always lean towards asking more interpretative questions, as I've found that to work better for me, even if a nice, straightforward yes or no would be appreciated.

I mention this because it occurs to me that you could adapt it to the Ogham fairly straightforwardly, especially if you use sticks instead of cards (I've thought about making a set out of popsicle sticks, which I did for my daughter's first Runestaves). Even with cards, though, you could pick a set number of cards to draw (say, five to fit with the symbolism of the Ogham), then interpret more uprights than reverses as "Yes." Like Thorsson said about the Runes, it might be that you have to have a good "feel" for your sticks/deck before this would work. I haven't tried this, though, so I don't know whether it would work at all!

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Ogham Interpretation
replies.content:
Thank you. I will try this approach.

Rooby

replies.title: Re: Ogham Interpretation
replies.content: The way I ask a yes/no question is to announce to the cards of any deck I am using, before I shuffle them, what the question is and that an upright card equals yes, a reversed card equals no. Then I shuffle as usual, cut the deck as usual, and turn over the top card. If it's upright, the answer is yes; if reversed, it's no. I interpret the card itself as telling me why the answer is yes or no.

We encounter evil impulses not so that we should fall in with them, but so that we can use them as "thrust blocks," sources of resistance to allow us to climb above them. If you have an evil desire, that's a form of negative evil, and by knowing the desire and then refusing it you grow in strength and wisdom. If you submit to it instead, that becomes positive evil, and then you generate bad karma and get to suffer the consequences. You have to experience the desire, but you don't have to act on it!

comments.title: Aphantasia
comments.content: Apologies if you have answered this previously, if there is a way to go about searching past Magic Monday topics it has escaped my notice. I am wondering if you have any input regarding aphantasia, the inability to form mental images. For quite some time now I have considered myself to have aphantasia, as I fit the criteria of being generally unable to bring any kind of imagery into mind. That being said, my mother recalls my having been quite imaginative as a child, and I my experience of dreaming does involve an impression of visual imagery, so I am wondering if perhaps it is less of a constitutional lack of capacity and more of a muscle which has suffered atrophy. Regardless, in my reading so far of your work, it seems that imagination holds quite an important place among the tools of occult practice. Have you encountered any practicing occultists who share such a difficulty, or who have overcome such? Do you know of any methods for strengthening one's ability to visualize? Thanks in advance for your assistance!

replies.title: Re: Aphantasia
replies.content:
Okay, this is complicated. First, a lot of people think they have aphantasia because they can't create mental images as vivid as a movie on a screen. Very, very few people can do this. Think about your bedroom. Do you remember where your bed is, and where the window or windows are. Think about the place where you work. What does it look like out front? If you can remember these things your imagination is strong enough to do magic.

Second, visual imagery isn't the only kind that matters. Bring all the imaginary senses into play. One trick I learned long ago is to feel, as well as see, the symbols I'm drawing in the air. I imagine a pentagram as being made of five bars of golden metal, cold to the touch and very smooth, which I feel with my fingertips as I slide them over the surface. (My pentagram rituals became dramatically stronger when I started doing that.)

Third, most people's imaginations have atrophied badly because they spend so much time staring into screens. Regular practice will fix that. Think about a red rubber ball. Don't "visualize" it, just think about it. Imagine yourself bouncing it off the floor; think about what it feels like when it hits your hand, and the sound it makes when it hits the floor. Do other exercises like that. Imagine yourself walking into the kitchen, getting a beer, and coming back to your chair. Come up with plenty of others. You'll do fine.

For what it's worth, I had a very poor visual imagination when I started doing this work; I naturally think in spoken language, not in imagery. Regular practice did the trick.

replies.title: Re: Aphantasia
replies.content:
Hi OP,

it sounds like we're similar when it comes to visual imagery/aphantasia. I worried about this too starting pathworking, but here is what I did: Take it slow. You'll have good days when images come and other days when they don't. Don't expect anything or be over critical - I could always sense concepts (as in "there is a rock ahead of me") but not always see them. Occasionally I get flashes of things or my immediate surroundings. Very rarely I get to see as if with my material senses. The first time that happened was amazing! Good luck :)

replies.title: Re: werewolves/lycanthropy
replies.content:
From a magical perspective, it makes perfect sense. Eliphas Levi argued that the loups-garous in early modern France practiced etheric projection -- their ordinary bodies went into trance while a nearly physical body, able to affect matter directly, roamed the night. The etheric tides are always at their height when the Moon is full.

It's occurred to me that the older tradition may have involved taking on an etheric body surrounding the material one -- that would make sense of the ulfedhnar and berserkr warriors who were seen as terrifying, shadowy half-animal figures. A full moon would be less necessary but still very helpful for that.

comments.title: Divination on the future
comments.content:
I would love to know on how to divine on the future, specifically the fate of the world in the next 2 or 3 years. Because divination on queries on the future are possible, initially I thought to seek out tarot readers for hire who may agree to this but there is practically no way to verify the readings.

John Michael Greer, I've seen you say that a glimpse into the future is possible for a powerful occultist. Daniel from the Old Testament was able to do exactly this.

How is this done?

replies.title: Re: Divination on the future
replies.content:
Well, actually, no -- the Book of Daniel was written many centuries after the events that it chronicled, and it used the setting of the Babylonian captivity to conceal a set of prophecies aimed at encouraging Jewish resistance to the Greek Seleucid government. There have been remarkable prophecies of the future, including some in the Christian tradition, but that's not one of them.

More broadly speaking, you've got two options, the way of the prophet and the way of the diviner. Prophets get their messages from gods, and the gods decide to send the message -- the prophet doesn't get to choose. Diviners do choose, but there's one simple but profoundly difficult requirement for good political divination: you have to stop caring about the outcome. As long as you have any emotional investment in how things are going to turn out, that's going to distort your vision, and you'll end up along with all those astrologers who predicted a Kamala Harris victory in the long, long list of people who got the future wrong.

replies.title: Re: Divination on the future
replies.content: Reminds me of how the astrologer Carter predicted peace in Europe on the eve of World War II because of his sympathies towards the Nazi racial ideology.

replies.title: Re: Divination on the future
replies.content: That's an example that's been much on my mind since I found out about it. It took astrology a generation to recover from that embarrassing failure.
